devotional.title: The Building Blocks of Mathematics
devotional.content:
{AtlasWarmJ}{esus}
told a parable about two builders. One built his house on rock; when the 
storms came, it stood firm. The other built on sand; when the rains fell, 
the house collapsed. The difference was the foundation.

In mathematics, whole numbers are our rock. Everything you will learn 
in pre-algebra—fractions, decimals, negative numbers, equations—builds 
upon the foundation of whole number arithmetic. If you understand place 
value, if you can add and multiply with confidence, if you know the 
order of operations by heart, then you are ready for whatever comes next.

The properties you learned in this chapter—commutativity, associativity, 
distributivity—are not arbitrary rules. They are truths about the 
nature of numbers, as reliable as the rising of the sun. Whether you 
are balancing a checkbook, calculating the distance to the stars, or 
designing a bridge, these same properties hold.

Prime numbers, those indivisible atoms of arithmetic, reveal that even 
the simplest numbers harbor deep mysteries. Mathematicians have studied 
primes for over two thousand years and continue to discover new truths 
about them. The order hidden in the whole numbers points to a deeper 
order underlying all of creation.

Take time to master this foundation. Return to this chapter whenever 
you feel uncertain. The skills you have developed here will serve you 
well in all your mathematical studies.
devotional.reflection:
The Fundamental Theorem of Arithmetic tells us 
that every whole number can be uniquely decomposed into prime factors. 
This means there is one and only one way to build each number from 
its prime building blocks. What might this uniqueness suggest about 
the nature of mathematical truth? About the mind that created it?

epigraph.text:
Whatever you do, work at it with all your heart, 
as working for the Lord, not for human masters.

content.text:
This chapter has laid the foundation for your study of pre-algebra. 
You have reviewed place value, practiced the four operations, learned 
the order of operations, and explored factors and multiples. These 
skills will be used throughout the course and beyond.
Match each term with its definition.

items: Place value A. A number with exactly two factors
Expanded form B. The value of a digit based on its position
Exponent C. A number that divides evenly into another
Factor D. Writing a number as $2{,}000 + 500 + 30 + 7$
Multiple E. Tells how many times to multiply a base
Prime number F. A product of a number and a whole number
Composite number G. A number with more than two factors
GCF H. The largest factor two numbers share
The digit in the ten-thousands place
The value of the digit 7
The place value of the digit 3

content.text: Write in standard form: Nine million, two hundred six thousand, fifteen.

items: $(8 \times 5) \times 2 = 8 \times (5 \times 2)$
hundred **(b)** thousand **(c)** ten-thousand
$4{,}827 + 3{,}156$ (to nearest thousand)
$67 \times 43$ (to nearest ten)
$5{,}847 \div 59$ (use compatible numbers)

content.text:
A student calculated $89 \times 52 = 4,528$. Use estimation to check 
if this is reasonable.
Find the GCF and LCM of 36 and 48.

A rectangular garden measures 24 feet by 18 feet. Find the perimeter 
and area of the garden.

Markers come in packs of 8, and pencils come in packs of 12. What is 
the smallest number of each type you could buy to have the same number 
of markers and pencils?

A theater has 28 rows with 45 seats per row. How many total seats are 
there?

If you have \$5,000 and spend \$1,847, then receive \$523 as a gift, 
how much money do you have?
